snippet.textDisplay: The delay in install and setup is likely LATENCY. So every interface and code has steps that have been implemented in a way that takes time. So talking between the phone and projector likely has some waits; either to mitigate loss or because they did. And the. Without an adequate software analyst they just paste the code in and each step takes time. It’s unlikely if developed for purpose any of the type of functions would take much time. I’d say it’s doing almost nothing. So basically bad programming, and either intentional delay or badly designed interfaces.
snippet.textOriginal: The delay in install and setup is likely LATENCY. So every interface and code has steps that have been implemented in a way that takes time. snippet.textDisplay:
So...for $2700 or less, I could have an 83" OLED from Samsung, or if I don't care if it's garbage (cause it's still way better than this projector) a 100" Hisense mini-QLED.

I'm just really not sure what the point of this product is. Seriously, unless it needs to be portable or you're paranoid about someone smashing into your TV, what is it they're trying to sell me on?

It can do a 200" screen in anything above true perfect darkness (and a perfectly hung, totally opaque white screen I'm sure). And since no one is going to have a 20-foot wide open wall in a room that seats viewers 50 feet away and which will also be kept in total blackness, 200" is the achievable max screen size and even that will be tough. Judging from this review, running it at maybe 100" in a pretty dim room, it looked like garbage because it can't actually use max brightness, though. So the realistic max size is probably 100" in a room that is nearly (but not quite) totally dark.

Or I can get a 100" Hisense that is garbage by TV standards, but still has better virtually everything than this projector and can be used no matter what the room lighting is.
snippet.textOriginal:

snippet.textDisplay: I don't get why people mention things you don't get with a projector that you get with an OLED or MicroLED or something... Or sure, it's good to adjust people's expectations, but... People don't get projectors for color accuracy or specular highlights - they get them for SCREEN SIZE. Becuase that's what makes cinema. Then people counter with 116 inch TV's that are available these days... Great for those few who can afford them. Simply put, if you intend to watch in full daylight or with the livingroom lit like a mall, then a projector is just not for you. Not even 5700 lumens and the most expensive screen in the world will save you.
snippet.textOriginal: I don't get why people mention things you don't get with a projector that you get with an OLED or MicroLED or something... Or sure, it's good to adjust people's expectations, but... People don't get projectors for color accuracy or specular highlights - they get them for SCREEN SIZE. Becuase that's what makes cinema. Then people counter with 116 inch TV's that are available these days... Great for those few who can afford them. Simply put, if you intend to watch in full daylight or with the livingroom lit like a mall, then a projector is just not for you. Not even 5700 lumens and the most expensive screen in the world will save you.
